Real Analysis

p-Series, Proper Divergence, and Limits of Functions

p-Series, Proper Divergence, and Limits of Functions

p-Series Partial Sums

Lecture 12 showed the harmonic series diverges by proving its partial sums fail to be Cauchy. This raises a natural question: what happens if we weaken each term from 1/k1/k1/k to 1/kp1/k^p1/kp? The answer turns out to hinge entirely on whether p>1p > 1p>1 or p≤1p \le 1p≤1.

Recall: The Harmonic Series Diverges

For the harmonic series partial sums

xn=∑k=1n1k,x_n = \sum_{k=1}^n \frac1k,xn​=k=1∑n​k1​,

we showed in Lecture 12 (via the Cauchy criterion) that xn→∞x_n \to \inftyxn​→∞ — the series diverges.

p-Series Test (Partial Sums)

For p>1p > 1p>1, the sequence of partial sums

xn(p)=∑k=1n1kpx_n(p) = \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{1}{k^p}xn​(p)=k=1∑n​kp1​

is convergent. For p≤1p \le 1p≤1, it is divergent.

We give two independent proofs of convergence for p>1p>1p>1: one using the Monotone Convergence Theorem (Lecture 8) together with an integral bound, and one using the Cauchy criterion (Lecture 12) directly. Comparing them highlights why the Cauchy approach demands more care.

Proof 1: Integral Comparison + Monotone Convergence

Proof

Monotonicity. Since every term 1/kp>01/k^p > 01/kp>0, the partial sums xn(p)x_n(p)xn​(p) are increasing.

Boundedness. For the decreasing function f(x)=x−pf(x) = x^{-p}f(x)=x−p with p>1p>1p>1, comparing the sum to an integral gives

∑k=1n1kp≤11p+∫1ndxxp≤1+∫1∞dxxp.\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{1}{k^p} \le \frac{1}{1^p} + \int_1^n \frac{dx}{x^p} \le 1 + \int_1^\infty \frac{dx}{x^p}.k=1∑n​kp1​≤1p1​+∫1n​xpdx​≤1+∫1∞​xpdx​.

Computing the improper integral,

∫1∞x−p dx=[x1−p1−p]1∞=1p−1\int_1^\infty x^{-p}\,dx = \left[\frac{x^{1-p}}{1-p}\right]_1^\infty = \frac{1}{p-1}∫1∞​x−pdx=[1−px1−p​]1∞​=p−11​

(finite precisely because p>1p > 1p>1, so x1−p→0x^{1-p} \to 0x1−p→0 as x→∞x\to\inftyx→∞). Hence xn(p)≤1+1p−1x_n(p) \le 1 + \dfrac{1}{p-1}xn​(p)≤1+p−11​, so (xn(p))(x_n(p))(xn​(p)) is bounded above.

By the Monotone Convergence Theorem (Lecture 8), an increasing sequence bounded above converges.

Proof 2: Cauchy Criterion via Integral Tail Bound

A tempting shortcut for verifying the Cauchy condition directly is to bound the tail sum by its largest term times the number of terms. This fails, and understanding why motivates the correct approach.

[!?] Why the Naive Bound Fails A tempting but incorrect step is

∑k=m+1n1kp≤(1(m+3)p)⋅m.\sum_{k=m+1}^n \frac{1}{k^p} \le \left(\frac{1}{(m+3)^p}\right)\cdot m.k=m+1∑n​kp1​≤((m+3)p1​)⋅m.

This fails for two reasons:

  • The number of terms in the sum is n−mn-mn−m, not mmm — and it depends on nnn, which is exactly the dependence we need to eliminate for a Cauchy bound.
  • Since 1/kp1/k^p1/kp is decreasing, 1(m+3)p\dfrac{1}{(m+3)^p}(m+3)p1​ is smaller than most terms in the sum, so multiplying it by a term count underestimates the sum rather than bounding it from above. A valid upper bound for a decreasing sequence must instead replace each term by an earlier, larger one — but doing so naively still leaves a factor of (n−m)(n-m)(n−m) depending on nnn.

The correct fix is to bound the tail sum by an integral whose value depends only on mmm, not on nnn — exactly the technique from Proof 1, now applied to a tail rather than the whole sum.

Proof (Cauchy)

For m<nm < nm<n,

∣xn(p)−xm(p)∣=∑k=m+1n1kp≤∫mndxxp≤∫m∞dxxp=m1−pp−1.|x_n(p) - x_m(p)| = \sum_{k=m+1}^n \frac{1}{k^p} \le \int_m^n \frac{dx}{x^p} \le \int_m^\infty \frac{dx}{x^p} = \frac{m^{1-p}}{p-1}.∣xn​(p)−xm​(p)∣=k=m+1∑n​kp1​≤∫mn​xpdx​≤∫m∞​xpdx​=p−1m1−p​.

Given ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0, choose MMM so that M1−pp−1<ϵ\dfrac{M^{1-p}}{p-1} < \epsilonp−1M1−p​<ϵ — possible since p>1p > 1p>1 makes m1−p→0m^{1-p} \to 0m1−p→0 as m→∞m \to \inftym→∞. Then for all m≥Mm \ge Mm≥M and n>mn > mn>m, ∣xn(p)−xm(p)∣<ϵ|x_n(p) - x_m(p)| < \epsilon∣xn​(p)−xm​(p)∣<ϵ. Thus (xn(p))(x_n(p))(xn​(p)) is Cauchy, and by the Cauchy Convergence Criterion (Lecture 12), it converges.

Monotonicity in the Exponent

Having settled convergence for p>1p>1p>1 and divergence for p≤1p\le 1p≤1, it is worth noting how the partial sums themselves compare across different exponents — term by term, a smaller exponent always produces a larger sum.

Partial-Sum Ordering in ppp

If 0<p<q0 < p < q0<p<q, then for every nnn,

∑k=1n1kp≥∑k=1n1kq.\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{1}{k^p} \ge \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{1}{k^q}.k=1∑n​kp1​≥k=1∑n​kq1​.

In particular,

∑k=1n1kp<1≥∑k=1n1k≥∑k=1n1kp>1.\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{1}{k^{p<1}} \ge \sum_{k=1}^n \frac1k \ge \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{1}{k^{p>1}}.k=1∑n​kp<11​≥k=1∑n​k1​≥k=1∑n​kp>11​.
What this comparison does and doesn’t tell us

This ordering alone does not prove convergence or divergence — a sequence can be termwise larger than a divergent one and still diverge, or termwise smaller than a convergent one and still converge in general. Its value here is calibration: it confirms that p=1p=1p=1 sits exactly at the boundary suggested by the p-Series Test, with every p<1p<1p<1 case dominating the harmonic series (and hence also diverging) and every p>1p>1p>1 case dominated by it.

Properly Divergent Sequences

Until now, “divergent” has simply meant “not convergent” — a catch-all category including oscillation, unboundedness, and more. We now isolate a well-behaved kind of divergence: sequences that grow (or shrink) without bound in a definite direction.

Monotonicity vs. “Tending to Infinity”

A sequence may tend to +∞+\infty+∞ without being monotone. For example, xn=n+(−1)nx_n = n + (-1)^nxn​=n+(−1)n is not monotone (it alternately overshoots and undershoots), yet lim⁡n→∞xn=∞\lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = \inftylimn→∞​xn​=∞.

Definitions

Proper Divergence

Let (xn)(x_n)(xn​) be a real sequence.

  • xn→∞x_n \to \inftyxn​→∞ if for every α>0\alpha > 0α>0 there exists NNN such that n≥N  ⟹  xn>αn \ge N \implies x_n > \alphan≥N⟹xn​>α.
  • xn→−∞x_n \to -\inftyxn​→−∞ if −xn→∞-x_n \to \infty−xn​→∞.
  • (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is properly divergent if xn→∞x_n \to \inftyxn​→∞ or xn→−∞x_n \to -\inftyxn​→−∞.

Example: Exponential Growth

Exponential Sequences Diverge

If c>1c > 1c>1, then cn→∞c^n \to \inftycn→∞.

This is the natural counterpart to the decaying geometric sequence result of Lecture 9 (0<b<1  ⟹  bn→00 < b < 1 \implies b^n \to 00<b<1⟹bn→0); here the base exceeds 111 instead, and Bernoulli’s inequality supplies the needed lower bound.

Proof

Write c=1+ac = 1+ac=1+a with a>0a > 0a>0. By Bernoulli’s inequality, (1+a)n≥1+na>na(1+a)^n \ge 1 + na > na(1+a)n≥1+na>na. Given any α>0\alpha > 0α>0, choose n>α/an > \alpha/an>α/a (Archimedean property) to get

cn>(1+a)n>na>α.c^n > (1+a)^n > na > \alpha.cn>(1+a)n>na>α.
Reciprocal Tends to Zero

If xn→∞x_n \to \inftyxn​→∞ or xn→−∞x_n \to -\inftyxn​→−∞, and xn≠0x_n \neq 0xn​=0 eventually, then 1xn→0\dfrac{1}{x_n} \to 0xn​1​→0.

The converse need not hold: xn=(−1)nnx_n = (-1)^n nxn​=(−1)nn satisfies 1/xn→01/x_n \to 01/xn​→0 but is not properly divergent — it oscillates without bound rather than heading consistently toward +∞+\infty+∞ or −∞-\infty−∞.

Divergence Tests

The exponential example above worked directly from the definition. The next three theorems give more efficient tools: reducing proper divergence to boundedness for monotone sequences, and comparing sequences against ones already known to diverge.

Divergence of Monotone Sequences

A monotone sequence is properly divergent if and only if it is unbounded.

Proof

If (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is increasing and unbounded, then for any α\alphaα there exists nnn with xn>αx_n > \alphaxn​>α; monotonicity then gives xk≥xn>αx_k \ge x_n > \alphaxk​≥xn​>α for all k≥nk \ge nk≥n, so xk→∞x_k \to \inftyxk​→∞. If (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is decreasing and unbounded below, symmetrically xk→−∞x_k \to -\inftyxk​→−∞.

Conversely, if a monotone sequence tends to ±∞\pm\infty±∞, it is immediate from the definition that it cannot be bounded.

The other side of the Monotone Convergence Theorem

This theorem completes the picture from Lecture 8: a monotone sequence either converges (if bounded) or is properly divergent (if unbounded) — there is no third possibility, unlike for general sequences, which can oscillate without any limiting behavior at all.

Direct Comparison (Divergent Form)

Suppose xn≤ynx_n \le y_nxn​≤yn​ for all sufficiently large nnn.

  • If xn→∞x_n \to \inftyxn​→∞, then yn→∞y_n \to \inftyyn​→∞.
  • If yn→−∞y_n \to -\inftyyn​→−∞, then xn→−∞x_n \to -\inftyxn​→−∞.
Proof

Fix α>0\alpha > 0α>0. If xn→∞x_n \to \inftyxn​→∞, then for large nnn, xn>αx_n > \alphaxn​>α, so yn≥xn>αy_n \ge x_n > \alphayn​≥xn​>α as well, giving yn→∞y_n \to \inftyyn​→∞. The second case follows the same way after negating.

Limit Comparison (Divergent Form)

If lim⁡n→∞xnyn=L\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{x_n}{y_n} = Ln→∞lim​yn​xn​​=L with L>0L > 0L>0, then xn→∞x_n \to \inftyxn​→∞ if and only if yn→∞y_n \to \inftyyn​→∞ (and similarly for −∞-\infty−∞, when signs are consistent).

Proof

Take ϵ=L/2\epsilon = L/2ϵ=L/2 in the definition of the limit of xn/ynx_n/y_nxn​/yn​: there exists NNN such that for n≥Nn \ge Nn≥N,

L2<xnyn<3L2.\frac{L}{2} < \frac{x_n}{y_n} < \frac{3L}{2}.2L​<yn​xn​​<23L​.

Multiplying through by yny_nyn​ (assuming yn>0y_n>0yn​>0 eventually, which the ratio’s convergence to L>0L>0L>0 forces) gives L2yn<xn<3L2yn\dfrac{L}{2}y_n < x_n < \dfrac{3L}{2}y_n2L​yn​<xn​<23L​yn​. If yn→∞y_n \to \inftyyn​→∞, the left inequality forces xn→∞x_n \to \inftyxn​→∞ by Direct Comparison. If xn→∞x_n \to \inftyxn​→∞, the right inequality forces yn→∞y_n \to \inftyyn​→∞ the same way.

Limits of Functions

We now shift from sequences to functions, defining what it means for f(x)f(x)f(x) to approach a limit as xxx approaches a point ccc. The definition will closely mirror the ε\varepsilonε–NNN definition of sequential convergence, with δ\deltaδ playing the role that NNN played before — but it first requires identifying exactly which points ccc a limit can sensibly be defined at.

Domain and Cluster Points

Consider fff defined on

A=(0,∞)∪{−1},f(x)={1/x,x>0,1,x=−1.A = (0,\infty) \cup \{-1\}, \qquad f(x) = \begin{cases} 1/x, & x > 0, \\ 1, & x = -1. \end{cases}A=(0,∞)∪{−1},f(x)={1/x,1,​x>0,x=−1.​

[!?] Can We Define lim⁡x→−1/2f(x)\lim_{x\to -1/2} f(x)limx→−1/2​f(x)? No. The point c=−12c = -\tfrac12c=−21​ is not approached by any other points of the domain AAA — nothing in AAA lies arbitrarily close to −12-\tfrac12−21​ besides possibly −12-\tfrac12−21​ itself, which isn’t even in AAA. Since no sequence of domain points other than ccc can approach ccc, “the limit at ccc” has no meaningful content here.

This motivates isolating exactly the points where a limit can be meaningfully defined: points that domain elements accumulate near.

Cluster Point

Let A⊆RA \subseteq \mathbb{R}A⊆R. A point c∈Rc \in \mathbb{R}c∈R is a cluster point of AAA if

∀ δ>0, ∃ x∈A∖{c} such that ∣x−c∣<δ.\forall\, \delta > 0,\ \exists\, x \in A \setminus \{c\} \text{ such that } |x - c| < \delta.∀δ>0, ∃x∈A∖{c} such that ∣x−c∣<δ.
Cluster Points of Various Sets
  • A finite set has no cluster points.
  • A={n:n∈N}A = \{n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}A={n:n∈N} has no cluster points in R\mathbb{R}R — consecutive integers never get arbitrarily close together.
  • A={1/n:n∈N}A = \{1/n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}A={1/n:n∈N} has a single cluster point, 000 (this is exactly the harmonic sequence from Lecture 4, whose infimum was shown to be 000).
  • A=(0,1)A = (0,1)A=(0,1) has cluster points filling [0,1][0,1][0,1] — including the endpoints, even though they aren’t in AAA itself.
  • For A=(0,∞)∪{−1}A = (0,\infty) \cup \{-1\}A=(0,∞)∪{−1} (our example above), the cluster points are [0,∞)[0,\infty)[0,∞); in particular −1-1−1 is isolated and is not a cluster point, matching the earlier observation.

Just as with subsequences (Lecture 9), cluster points admit a convenient sequential characterization — often easier to verify than the raw δ\deltaδ-definition.

Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points

A point c∈Rc \in \mathbb{R}c∈R is a cluster point of A⊆RA \subseteq \mathbb{R}A⊆R if and only if there exists a sequence (an)⊂A∖{c}(a_n) \subset A \setminus \{c\}(an​)⊂A∖{c} with an→ca_n \to can​→c.

Proof Sketch

If ccc is a cluster point, apply the definition with δ=1/n\delta = 1/nδ=1/n for each nnn to choose an∈A∖{c}a_n \in A\setminus\{c\}an​∈A∖{c} with ∣an−c∣<1/n|a_n - c| < 1/n∣an​−c∣<1/n; then an→ca_n \to can​→c by the Archimedean property.

Conversely, if such a sequence exists, then for any δ>0\delta>0δ>0, convergence of (an)(a_n)(an​) guarantees infinitely many terms within δ\deltaδ of ccc, so every δ\deltaδ-neighborhood of ccc meets A∖{c}A\setminus\{c\}A∖{c}.

Limit of a Function at a Cluster Point

With cluster points identified as the legitimate domain for taking limits, we now define the limit of a function itself — structurally identical to sequential convergence, with δ\deltaδ controlling closeness in xxx exactly as NNN controlled largeness in nnn.

Limit at a Cluster Point

Let A⊆RA \subseteq \mathbb{R}A⊆R and let ccc be a cluster point of AAA. A number LLL is the limit of f:A→Rf: A \to \mathbb{R}f:A→R at ccc if

∀ ϵ>0, ∃ δ>0 such that ∀ x∈A, 0<∣x−c∣<δ  ⟹  ∣f(x)−L∣<ϵ.\forall\, \epsilon > 0,\ \exists\, \delta > 0 \text{ such that } \forall\, x \in A,\ 0 < |x-c| < \delta \implies |f(x) - L| < \epsilon.∀ϵ>0, ∃δ>0 such that ∀x∈A, 0<∣x−c∣<δ⟹∣f(x)−L∣<ϵ.

We write lim⁡x→cf(x)=L\lim_{x\to c} f(x) = Llimx→c​f(x)=L.

Why 0<∣x−c∣0 < |x-c|0<∣x−c∣, not ∣x−c∣|x-c|∣x−c∣

The condition 0<∣x−c∣0 < |x-c|0<∣x−c∣ deliberately excludes x=cx = cx=c itself — the limit only concerns how fff behaves near ccc, not the value f(c)f(c)f(c) (which may not even be defined). This is why the definition requires ccc to be a cluster point of AAA: it guarantees points x≠cx \ne cx=c with x∈Ax \in Ax∈A exist arbitrarily close to ccc, so the condition 0<∣x−c∣<δ0<|x-c|<\delta0<∣x−c∣<δ is never vacuous.

Example: lim⁡x→c1x=1c\lim_{x\to c} \frac1x = \frac1climx→c​x1​=c1​ for c≠0c \neq 0c=0

This example mirrors the reciprocal limit law for sequences (Lecture 8, Exercise 5) almost step for step — the key algebraic manipulation, bounding ∣x∣|x|∣x∣ away from 000, is identical.

Continuity of x↦1/xx \mapsto 1/xx↦1/x Away from 000

If c≠0c \neq 0c=0 and A⊆R∖{0}A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}A⊆R∖{0} has ccc as a cluster point, then

lim⁡x→c1x=1c.\lim_{x\to c} \frac1x = \frac1c.x→clim​x1​=c1​.
Proof

For x≠0x \neq 0x=0,

∣1x−1c∣=∣x−c∣∣xc∣.\left|\frac1x - \frac1c\right| = \frac{|x-c|}{|xc|}.​x1​−c1​​=∣xc∣∣x−c∣​.

Choose

δ=min⁡{∣c∣2, ∣c∣2ϵ2}.\delta = \min\left\{\frac{|c|}{2},\ \frac{|c|^2\epsilon}{2}\right\}.δ=min{2∣c∣​, 2∣c∣2ϵ​}.

If 0<∣x−c∣<δ≤∣c∣/20 < |x-c| < \delta \le |c|/20<∣x−c∣<δ≤∣c∣/2, then by the reverse triangle inequality, ∣x∣≥∣c∣−∣x−c∣≥∣c∣/2|x| \ge |c| - |x-c| \ge |c|/2∣x∣≥∣c∣−∣x−c∣≥∣c∣/2. Hence

∣1x−1c∣=∣x−c∣∣xc∣≤∣x−c∣(∣c∣/2)∣c∣=2∣c∣2∣x−c∣<2∣c∣2⋅∣c∣2ϵ2=ϵ.\left|\frac1x-\frac1c\right| = \frac{|x-c|}{|xc|} \le \frac{|x-c|}{(|c|/2)|c|} = \frac{2}{|c|^2}|x-c| < \frac{2}{|c|^2}\cdot\frac{|c|^2\epsilon}{2} = \epsilon.​x1​−c1​​=∣xc∣∣x−c∣​≤(∣c∣/2)∣c∣∣x−c∣​=∣c∣22​∣x−c∣<∣c∣22​⋅2∣c∣2ϵ​=ϵ.

Therefore lim⁡x→c1/x=1/c\lim_{x\to c} 1/x = 1/climx→c​1/x=1/c.
